title: Qualify as an Enterprise land-and-expand opportunity and pus
trigger: An inbound lead where the immediate team is small but the parent company is large, and there is a hard deadline.

signals.name: The mismatch between a 40-person team and a 600-person company
signals.why_it_matters: A small team inside a big company with a deadline is almost always a beachhead — land the pilot, then expand. The deadline means they will actually buy, not just browse.

signals.name: a hard 6-week deadline
signals.why_it_matters: A small team inside a big company with a deadline is almost always a beachhead — land the pilot, then expand. The deadline means they will actually buy, not just browse.

signals.name: a fast, specific first reply
signals.why_it_matters: A small team inside a big company with a deadline is almost always a beachhead — land the pilot, then expand. The deadline means they will actually buy, not just browse.

preconditions: there is a named compelling event with a date
decision: Qualify as an Enterprise land-and-expand opportunity and push for a 30-minute scoping call this week, overriding the seat-count auto-routing.
rationale: A small team inside a big company with a deadline is almost always a beachhead — land the pilot, then expand. The deadline means they will actually buy, not just browse. Get a scoping call booked this week before the deadline pressure fades, and set up land-and-expand. Last year a 25-seat pilot at a 2,000-person firm became our largest manufacturing account — same pattern.

alternatives.option: or disqualify as too small. Both are wrong — self-serve wastes the expansion

reversal_conditions: If the parent company were actually small
or if there were no deadline
I would route it to SMB self-serve
cues_to_watch: they book the call within 48 hours
watch whether a VP or procurement joins
